skill.name: pexoai-agent
skill.description: Create finished AI videos through Pexo from text, images, scripts, URLs, or audio. The skill uses shell helpers to create projects, upload media, send prompts, poll status, and deliver results.
skill.summary: Generate and manage Pexo AI video projects from an AI coding agent.
skill.author: pexoai

security_audit.summary: The static alerts are partly true positives: the skill runs bash scripts, sources a user config file, sends bearer-token API calls, uploads local media, and downloads signed assets. Many command, weak-crypto, and Windows SAM alerts are false positives from documentation, markdown examples, or ordinary shell parsing. No confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, but publication should require manual review and clear user warnings.

high_findings.title: Shell Config File Is Sourced
high_findings.description: The shared helper sources the configured Pexo config file as shell code. A modified config can execute commands in the agent environment when any script runs.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses source on a user-controlled config path. This is a real execution risk, although it is common for shell-based tooling.

high_findings.title: Instruction Priority Claim in Skill Prompt
high_findings.description: The skill text declares a language rule as highest priority and non-negotiable. This is not proven malicious, but it asserts instruction priority inside untrusted marketplace content.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording is explicit and high priority, but it only concerns response language and does not tell the agent to skip security checks or ignore platform policy.

medium_findings.title: Bearer Token Sent to Configured Endpoint
medium_findings.description: API helpers build an Authorization bearer header from PEXO_API_KEY and send it to PEXO_BASE_URL. This is required for Pexo, but endpoint configuration must be trusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key header and curl calls are direct. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so this is a controlled but sensitive network pattern.

medium_findings.title: Local Media Files Can Be Uploaded
medium_findings.description: The upload helper accepts a local file path and sends the file contents to a signed upload URL. This can expose sensitive files if the agent uploads the wrong path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data transfer is explicit and functional. It is legitimate for video generation, but it needs user consent and path review.

medium_findings.title: Signed Asset Downloads Written Locally
medium_findings.description: The asset helper downloads signed URLs into ~/.pexo/tmp or PEXO_TMP_DIR. This creates local copies that may persist after delivery.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly writes downloaded assets to disk. The risk depends on asset sensitivity and local file permissions.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Trigger Static Alerts
low_findings.description: Many external command and filesystem alerts occur in setup and troubleshooting documentation. These are user-facing examples rather than hidden execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown examples and explanatory text. They do not execute unless a user deliberately follows the setup steps.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Windows SAM Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and Windows SAM detections map to ordinary words, script descriptions, or API-key prefix examples. No cryptographic operation or SAM database access was found in the cited context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines do not reference Windows credential files or implement weak cryptography. They appear to be scanner keyword collisions.

dangerous_patterns.title: source on User-Configurable Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts execute shell content from PEXO_CONFIG or ~/.pexo/config, which is risky if that file is writable by an attacker.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct shell sourcing is present and semantically significant. Exploitability depends on config file integrity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated curl Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts use curl with an Authorization bearer header for Pexo API calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The curl calls are direct and include a bearer header helper. This is expected but security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Local File Upload Argument
dangerous_patterns.description: The upload script sends the file path supplied on the command line to remote storage after type checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code reads and uploads the provided path. This is a legitimate feature with data exposure risk.

content.value_statement: Video production often requires model choices, file handling, polling, and delivery steps. This skill coordinates Pexo projects from an AI agent and returns video links and files.
actual_capabilities: Creates new Pexo video projects from a short project name.
Uploads supported image, video, and audio files to a Pexo project.
Sends user prompts and asset references to the Pexo chat endpoint.
Polls project status and reports whether to wait, respond, deliver, reconnect, or retry.
Fetches final or preview asset URLs and caches downloaded files locally.
Runs a setup diagnostic for config, dependencies, network access, and API key validity.
limitations: Requires curl, jq, file, network access, and a valid PEXO_API_KEY.
Uploads local files selected by the agent or user to Pexo-controlled storage.
Uses shell scripts, so users should review commands before running them.
Video rendering can take minutes and may require paid credits.

use_cases.title: Create Product Ads
use_cases.description: Turn product photos or product URLs into short promotional videos for campaigns.

use_cases.title: Produce Social Shorts
use_cases.description: Generate vertical videos for TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts from a brief.

use_cases.title: Automate Video Drafts
use_cases.description: Use scripts to create, check, revise, and deliver Pexo video projects from a terminal workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 15 second video about our new coffee blend for Instagram Reels.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Product Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Image-to-video request with an uploaded asset
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this product photo to make a clean product ad with upbeat music and clear benefits.

prompt_templates.title: Revise an Existing Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Update a Pexo draft after preview
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the same project and make the opening faster, with more focus on the product in the first shot.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Full Campaign Variant
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three variants for the same product: one informative, one emotional, and one direct response.

output_examples.input: Create a product video from a shoe photo.
output_examples.output: The agent creates a project, uploads the photo, sends the brief, and returns a Pexo project link while rendering continues.
The agent fetches the final asset, provides the signed video URL, and reports the local cached file path when available.

output_examples.input: The preview is close, but make it brighter.
output_examples.output: The agent sends the revision to the existing project and resumes polling until Pexo asks for input or delivers a result.

best_practices: Review local file paths before uploading any media to Pexo.
Keep the Pexo API key in a restricted config file or environment variable.
Wait between project status checks to avoid duplicate work or extra costs.
anti_patterns: Do not upload private files unless the user clearly requested it.
Do not paste secrets or unrelated personal data into video prompts.
Do not retry credit failures until the account has available credits.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It creates Pexo video projects and helps deliver finished AI-generated videos.

faq.question: Does it require a Pexo account?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires a PEXO_API_KEY and a PEXO_BASE_URL value.

faq.question: Can it upload my files?
faq.answer: Yes. The upload script can send selected local media files to a signed upload URL.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses bash scripts with curl, jq, file, mktemp, and related utilities.

faq.question: Can it work without internet access?
faq.answer: No. The scripts need outbound HTTPS access to the configured Pexo service.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review prompts, files, and the configured endpoint before sending sensitive media or business data.
